**Acknowledgments:** The authors thank the residents of the Downtown
Eastside, whose lived experience is the foundation of this work. This
paper is dedicated to them---to the ones who stay, and to the truth of
what they endure.

# History of a Dopamine Rush

***The Coming Societal Withdrawal***

## Abstract

This paper argues that contemporary Western societies are experiencing
the initial stages of a collective psychological withdrawal from
consumerism, a system best understood not merely as an economic
arrangement but as a neurochemically engineered addiction. Drawing upon
neuroscience, historical analysis, and social psychology, we trace the
evolution of consumer culture from its origins in early modern trade to
its current manifestation as a digitally optimized dopamine-delivery
system. The "illusions" underpinning this system---infinite growth, the
equation of acquisition with fulfillment, and the attainability of the
aspirational lifestyle---are visibly crumbling under the weight of
ecological limits, psychological evidence, and economic reality. The
resulting "withdrawal syndrome" manifests as rising rates of anomie,
political rage, and compensatory digital addiction, while simultaneously
opening possibilities for post-consumerist modes of meaning-making
centered on sufficiency, experience, and community.

## 1. Introduction

Imagine a patient who has spent decades chasing a particular high. The
substance promised relief from boredom, anxiety, and the nagging sense
of inadequacy. For a time, it delivered. But gradually, the doses
required escalation. The old pleasures lost their power. The side
effects---clutter, debt, a pervasive emptiness---became impossible to
ignore. Now the supply is running thin, the promised effects have faded,
and the patient is left irritable, despairing, and desperate for
something---anything---to fill the void.

This patient is not an individual. It is us. It is the collective body
of modern consumer society.

The thesis of this paper is straightforward yet unsettling:
**consumerism functions as a socially engineered addiction, operating
through the hijacking of the brain's dopamine reward system. The
foundational promises upon which this system rests are now visibly
failing. As they crumble, societies are entering a painful period of
collective withdrawal, characterized by psychological distress,
political volatility, and a desperate search for substitute highs. Yet
within this crisis lies the potential for a fundamental reorientation of
human meaning and value.**

We will proceed in four movements. First, we examine the neurochemical
architecture of consumer desire, establishing the dopaminergic basis of
modern consumption. Second, we trace the historical construction of this
system, showing how it was deliberately cultivated over centuries.
Third, we diagnose the current moment as one of acute withdrawal,
cataloguing its symptoms across psychological and social domains.
Finally, we consider the possibilities latent in this crisis---the seeds
of a post-consumerist culture already visible on the margins.

## 2. The Dopamine Architecture of Desire

To understand consumerism as addiction, we must begin with the molecule
that makes it possible. Dopamine is frequently mischaracterized as the
"pleasure chemical." In truth, it is the molecule of **anticipation and
wanting**---the neurochemical signature of desire itself.

When an advertisement catches your eye, when you anticipate the arrival
of a package, when you scroll past an image of someone living a life
more beautiful than your own, your mesolimbic dopamine system activates.
This is not pleasure you are feeling; it is the promise of pleasure. The
late neuroscientist Read Montague and his colleagues at Virginia Tech
demonstrated in 2025 that emotionally charged marketing language
triggers distinct patterns of dopamine and serotonin release within
**sub-seconds**, well before conscious cognition can intervene. The
system is designed to bypass rationality entirely.

This is not accidental. A 2024 research topic in the journal *Frontiers*
explicitly identifies "valuation mechanisms and associated responses of
dopamine-rich brain areas as crucial correlates of consumption." The
mesolimbic system, the researchers note, serves as a "common mechanism
implicated in aspects of consumption as diverse as the experience of
aesthetic product packaging, the trade-off between brand and price, and
the role of status symbols."

The implication is profound: when we participate in consumer culture, we
are not making rational choices about utility. We are riding waves of
neurochemistry, engineered by parties with a vested interest in keeping
those waves coming.

## 3. The Historical Construction of the Addict

The addict is not born; she is made. So too with consumer society. The
historical record reveals a deliberate, centuries-long project to
transform human beings from producers oriented toward sufficiency into
consumers oriented toward perpetual desire.

### 3.1 The First Rush (17th-18th Centuries)

The story begins not with factories but with ships. The Dutch Golden Age
and the expansion of global trade introduced Europeans to novel
goods---sugar, tobacco, calico, porcelain, coffee, tea. These were not
mere commodities; they were vessels of exoticism, carrying the magic of
distant lands. To possess them was to participate in a wider, more
sophisticated world.

Economic historian Jan de Vries termed this period the "Industrious
Revolution." Households began reallocating labor---working harder and
longer---not to survive, but to earn the cash necessary to acquire these
new market goods. The first sacrifice for the drug had been made.

### 3.2 Engineering the Craving (19th Century)

The Industrial Revolution solved the problem of production but created a
new one: meaning. Factory-made goods were anonymous, stripped of the
aura of craftsmanship. How could they command desire?

The answer was the **brand**. Manufacturers learned to inject abstract
values into their products. A bar of soap became a promise of purity and
respectability. Quaker Oats acquired a human face. Simultaneously,
mass-circulation catalogues and magazines created the first "virtual"
shopping experiences. For the first time, one could desire an object one
had never touched.

T.J. Jackson Lears, in his seminal work "From Salvation to
Self-Realization," documents the cultural shift that accompanied these
changes. Between 1880 and 1930, advertising helped engineer a transition
from a "Protestant ethos of salvation through self-denial" to a
"therapeutic ethos stressing self-realization in this world."
Consumption was repositioned as the path to authentic selfhood.

### 3.3 The Maintenance Dose (20th Century)

Television represented the hypodermic needle of consumerism---a
constant, 24/7 drip of desire delivered directly into the domestic
space. The teenager was invented as a demographic whose sole purpose was
to consume with focused intensity. Planned obsolescence, articulated
explicitly by advertisers in the 1930s, ensured that satisfaction would
always remain just out of reach. The new model rendered the old one not
merely inferior but shameful.

By mid-century, the Cartesian mantra had quietly shifted. No longer "I
think, therefore I am," but rather **"I consume, therefore I am."**
Identity became something to be assembled from purchases.

### 3.4 The Digital Overdose (21st Century)

The smartphone represents the final refinement of the system. As
Stanford psychiatrist Anna Lembke observes, it functions as the
"modern-day hypodermic needle that delivers digital dopamine 24/7."

Social media platforms, shopping apps, and infinite scroll interfaces
are slot machines for the soul. The reward is unpredictable---a like, a
comment, a perfectly targeted advertisement for exactly the thing you
didn't know you wanted. This unpredictability is essential; it is what
makes the dopamine loop inescapable. We scroll not despite the boredom
but because of it, each swipe holding the potential for a hit.

## 4. The Crumbling Illusions

Every addiction rests on a set of enabling beliefs. For consumerism,
these beliefs have formed the foundation of social order for
generations. All are now visibly failing.

### 4.1 The Illusion of Infinite Growth

The first and most fundamental crack is ecological. Consumer capitalism
requires perpetual expansion. Yet it operates on a finite planet with
dwindling resources and accumulating waste. The climate crisis, mass
extinction, and plastic-choked oceans are not externalities; they are
the system's balance sheet coming due. The promise that everyone can
consume forever collides with the second law of thermodynamics.

### 4.2 The Illusion of Fulfillment

The psychological evidence is now overwhelming: beyond a relatively
modest threshold, increased consumption does not produce increased
happiness. The "hedonic treadmill" ensures that each acquisition resets
the baseline, leaving the consumer no more satisfied than before---and
often less so, as the gap between aspiration and reality widens.

Tim Kasser's edited volume *Psychology and Consumer Culture*
systematically documents the relationship between materialistic values
and decreased life satisfaction, poorer relationships, and reduced
community involvement. The very pursuit of the consumer dream undermines
the conditions for genuine well-being.

### 4.3 The Illusion of Attainability

The aspirational lifestyle sold by advertising has become financially
inaccessible to a growing portion of the population. Stagnant wages,
soaring housing costs, student debt, and precarious employment mean that
the promised dream---a home, a car, regular vacations, security---is for
many a cruel taunt rather than a realistic goal.

The result is a state of **frustrated desire**: the dopamine loop is
triggered constantly, but the reward never arrives. The addict is kept
in a state of perpetual anticipation without consummation.

### 4.4 The Illusion of Digital Substitution

We have attempted to replace physical consumption with its digital
shadow. Streaming, social media, and virtual experiences offer endless
content *about* life without the messiness of living it. But this only
accelerates the dopamine cycle while making it more abstract. The result
is a gnawing hunger for something tangible, something real, that the
digital realm cannot satisfy.

## 5. The Withdrawal Syndrome

When an addict's supply is cut off---whether by circumstance or by the
failure of the substance to deliver---the result is withdrawal. The
symptoms are not merely physical but psychological and behavioral. We
are witnessing them at societal scale.

### 5.1 Collective Anomie and Despair

The philosopher Charles Taylor speaks of the "malaise of
modernity"---the sense that life has lost its meaning despite---or
because of---its material abundance. If identity was constructed through
consumption, what remains when consumption no longer delivers? The
rising rates of depression, anxiety, and loneliness, particularly among
younger generations raised in the most intense phase of digital
consumerism, are not coincidental. They are the psychic cost of a failed
promise.

### 5.2 The Rage Response

Dopamine withdrawal is not merely sad; it is angry. The addict,
confronted with the failure of the substance, seeks someone to blame.
This manifests politically as scapegoating---of immigrants, of elites,
of foreign powers---and as attraction to authoritarian figures who
promise to restore a mythical past of plenty. The volatility of
contemporary politics is, in part, the thrashing of a society in
withdrawal.

### 5.3 The Search for Substitute Highs

The addict will seek any available substitute. We see this in:

- **Digital addiction:** Retreat into the very platforms that enabled
  the consumerist cycle.
- **Financial speculation:** The replacement of consumer gambling with
  market gambling, from meme stocks to cryptocurrency.
- **Outrage addiction:** Moral outrage delivered by 24-hour news and
  social media provides a potent, addictive sense of purpose and
  stimulation.

Stefano Passini, writing in *SAGE Journals*, characterizes contemporary
society as a "binge-consuming culture" exhibiting precisely these
features: present-time orientation, impulsiveness, and a narcissistic
preoccupation with self-gratification that leaves no room for sustained
meaning.

## 6. Beyond the Void: Emerging Alternatives

Withdrawal is a crisis, but it is also a necessary passage. The
crumbling of consumerism opens space for alternative modes of
meaning-making already visible on the margins.

### 6.1 Anti-Consumers and the Pleasures of Production

A growing cohort, documented in both academic and popular literature,
derives its dopamine not from acquisition but from creation, repair, and
maintenance. They fix what is broken, make what they need, and find
satisfaction in competence rather than ownership. The pleasure is not in
the new but in the durable; not in the disposable but in the cared-for.

### 6.2 The Shift from Having to Being

The turn toward experience over ownership---travel, learning,
relationships, craft---represents a reorientation of value. These goods
cannot be accumulated or displayed, but they can be integrated into the
self in ways that possessions cannot.

### 6.3 The Ethics of Sufficiency

The most profound shift may be philosophical: from the endless pursuit
of "more" to the cultivation of "enough." This is not asceticism but a
recognition that meaning lies not in the quantity of possessions but in
the quality of attention, relationship, and presence.

## 7. Conclusion

We have told the story of a society that learned to engineer its own
dopamine and, in doing so, built a civilization on a foundation of sand.
The illusions that sustained it---infinite growth, the fulfillment of
acquisition, the attainability of the dream---are crumbling before our
eyes. The withdrawal is painful, disorienting, and sometimes dangerous.
It manifests as despair, as rage, as desperate grasping for substitutes.

But withdrawal is also the precondition for recovery. The crack in the
Crystal Palace lets in light. If the old sources of meaning are failing,
new ones are struggling to be born---sources rooted not in consumption
but in creation, not in possession but in presence, not in the endless
pursuit of more but in the difficult, liberating discovery of enough.

The end of consumerism, if that is what we are witnessing, is not the
end of the world. It is the end of a world---a world that promised much
and delivered little, that stimulated endlessly and satisfied never.
What comes next is not yet determined. It depends on whether we can
navigate the withdrawal with awareness rather than denial, and whether
we can recognize, in the rubble of the old illusions, the seeds of
something worth building.

## References

- de Vries, J. (2008). *The Industrious Revolution: Consumer Behavior
  and the Household Economy, 1650 to the Present*. Cambridge University
  Press.
- Kasser, T. (Ed.). (2002). *Psychology and Consumer Culture: The
  Struggle for a Good Life in a Materialistic World*. American
  Psychological Association.
- Lears, T. J. J. (1983). From Salvation to Self-Realization:
  Advertising and the Therapeutic Roots of the Consumer Culture,
  1880-1930. In R. W. Fox & T. J. J. Lears (Eds.), *The Culture of
  Consumption*.
- Lembke, A. (2021). *Dopamine Nation: Finding Balance in the Age of
  Indulgence*. Dutton.
- Montague, R., et al. (2025). \[Study on sub-second dopamine and
  serotonin responses to marketing language\]. *Proceedings of the
  National Academy of Sciences*.
- Passini, S. (2013). A binge-consuming culture: The effect of
